methods_all.name: Historical Likelihood Model
methods_all.description: Calculates likelihood of internal conflict based on share of economic measures worse than threshold.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Likelihood increases from 12% to 30% as the share of poor economic measures rises from <40% to >80%

methods_all.name: EQUITY RETURN INDEX (USD)
methods_all.description: Tracks equity performance of USA, GBR, DEU, JPN in USD
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses historical data to compare and contrast the impact of wartime economic controls on equity markets.

methods_all.name: Historical Return Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing past returns to understand worst-case scenarios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Annualized returns assume a full 10-year period even if markets closed during the decade.

methods_all.name: Tax Impact Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the impact of taxes on rolling 20-year S&P total returns.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Applies a 26% income tax rate at the end of each period for 401(k) and separately taxes dividends and capital gains in brokerage accounts.

methods_all.name: Bank Reserve Ratio Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing the ratio of precious metals to monetary liabilities.

methods_all.name: Historical Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing past events to predict future trends.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Identify patterns in historical data such as wars, economic output, and technological innovation.

methods_all.name: FX rate analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing the USD/GBP exchange rate and its relation to gold.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: USD/GBP (INV) GBR FX VS GOLD (£/OZ, INV)

heuristics_all: Dynamics to pay attention to include self-interest, desire for wealth and power, learning from history, multigenerational psychological cycles
Time frame of decision making varies between individual (short-term) and country (long-term)
Leadership quality affects openness to global thinking
Type 3 fiat money maximizes credit but minimizes credibility
Historical context shows the impact of wars and economic crises on currency values
Historical currency and gold returns can inform current investment decisions
Major economic events like the end of Bretton Woods impact long-term financial trends
Global RGDP is primarily a mix of European countries before 1870 due to limited reliable data coverage across other countries before that point.
Risk of conflict nearly triples when all economic measures are problematic
Avoid counting periods of economic downturn due to internal conflict.
Equities perform well during periods of Axis dominance but suffer when Allied powers gain control.
Historical asset returns can provide insights into future performance under similar macroeconomic conditions.
Equities tend to outperform bonds and cash during periods of economic growth, while bonds may perform better in inflationary or recessionary environments.
Historical performance data can provide insights into long-term investment trends and risks.
Consider the impact of geopolitical events on asset classes in specific countries.
Consider historical periods of extreme economic and political instability when assessing potential risks for investment portfolios.
Hyperinflation, debt repudiation, and market closures can lead to significant negative real returns over extended periods.
Identify periods of wealth confiscation and strict/rising capital controls
Consider historical context for investment decisions
Historical stock market closures correlate with major violent conflicts and economic crises.
Economic sanctions can restrict cross-border investment flows, impacting global markets.
Tax drag significantly reduces real return over long periods
Historical asset allocation trends can inform current investment strategies
Historical context can provide insights into current economic conditions
Understanding past world orders helps predict shifts in global power
Historical context can provide insights into economic cycles and shifts in global power
Historical context can provide insights into economic cycles.
Major wars often lead to significant shifts in global power dynamics.
Historical bank reserve ratios can indicate periods of financial instability and devaluation
Past performance of major empires can indicate future global power dynamics.
Economic and military strength correlate with reserve currency status.
Between 1850 and 1914, about 60% of world trade was denominated in pound sterling
UK's wealth gap (top 1% wealth share) increased significantly from 1800 to 1920
Economic and military competition can lead to significant shifts in global power dynamics
Education investment correlates with future economic and technological advancements
Deterioration of current account is linked to economic recessions
Sharp interest rate hikes are often seen during currency defense
Central banks' reserve policies can significantly impact currency stability.
Empire Score is a composite measure comparing global power metrics.
Identify major wars and their impact on empires
Track innovation periods as they correlate with economic dominance
Historical data can provide insights into economic cycles and political trends.
Economic indicators like GDP, unemployment rates, and central bank balance sheets are crucial for understanding macroeconomic conditions.
Historical context can inform future economic trends
China's relative standing in world history influences its current and future status
key_concepts: Big Cycle of External Order and Disorder
Big Cycle of Internal Order and Disorder
Relative shares of world manufacturing output
share of global debt and transactions
regime_conditions: Global RGDP mix dominated by European countries before 1870 due to data limitations
Global economic shifts affecting reserve currencies
Global wars impact economic output and technological innovation.
Historical macroeconomic regimes affecting equity, bond, and cash performance
Impact of major historical events (WWI, WWII, Dot-com bubble) on financial markets
Shifts in global power dynamics influence financial centers and reserve currencies.
periods of internal conflict and the five years following
takeaways: The Changing World Order is influenced by cycles of internal and external order, technological innovation, economic output, trade dynamics, military strength, and cultural openness.
The changing world order and historical events significantly impact the value and credibility of different types of money.
Historical real returns of major countries since 1850 reveal the impact of significant economic events on long-term investment performance.
Global RGDP before 1870 is heavily biased towards European countries due to data limitations.
The likelihood of internal conflict nearly triples as the share of poor economic measures increases from less than 40% to over 80%
Avoid analyzing economic data during periods immediately affected by internal conflicts.
Equity markets are significantly impacted by wartime economic controls and the shifting balance of power during World War II.
Historical asset returns across different macroeconomic regimes can inform strategic portfolio diversification.
Historical data shows significant variability in portfolio performance across different countries and time periods due to geopolitical events.
Historical analysis of major countries shows that periods of extreme political and economic instability can result in catastrophic negative real returns for investors over a 20-year window.
Historical periods of wealth confiscation and strict capital controls are marked by extensive government intervention in private assets and restrictions on international investment.
Stock market closures and major violent conflicts have historically impacted global economic stability.
Taxes can significantly reduce the average annual total and real returns over a 20-year period.
Analyzing historical trends can provide valuable insights into current and future economic conditions.
The historical context shows how shifts in innovation, empire size, and economic policies can lead to changes in global power dynamics.
The Dutch Golden Age saw the rise and fall of economic dominance through major historical events.
The Bank of Amsterdam's reserve ratio sharply declined post-Fourth Anglo-Dutch War, indicating financial instability and leading to significant devaluation of the Dutch guilder.
Historical analysis of major empires' performance can provide insights into future geopolitical and economic trends.
Between 1850 and 1914, about 60% of world trade was denominated in pound sterling
Germany's significant investment in education and manufacturing during the late 19th century contributed to its rise as a global power, surpassing Britain and France.
Sharp interest rate hikes and the decline of gold reserves are indicative of currency defense measures during economic downturns.
Central banks' decisions on holding or selling sterling reserves were critical in determining the pound's stability and global influence.
The rise and decline of empires, particularly the United States, is influenced by major wars, innovation periods, and economic cycles.
Historical economic and political data can be used to predict future trends in GDP growth, unemployment rates, and income distribution.
China's historical cycles and monetary transitions provide insights into its current economic position and future prospects.
